title: EQ Eight — Frequency Carving
summary: Use EQ Eight to carve space in your mix and remove mud. Every track in UK garage needs at least a high-pass filter.
steps: Add EQ Eight to any track you want to shape
Double-click a band dot to activate it, or click the numbered band buttons at the bottom
For most non-kick tracks: activate band 1, switch to High Pass (HP), set frequency to 60–120Hz to remove sub rumble
For hi-hats or cymbals: add a Low Pass (LP) on band 8 around 12–15kHz to soften harsh highs
To cut mud (200–400Hz): activate a bell band, set to around 300Hz, pull gain down 3–6dB
Toggle the 'Spectrum' button (bottom right) to see a live frequency display and identify problem frequencies
tips: Narrow Q (high value) = surgical cut on a specific frequency. Wide Q = broader, smoother shape
Cut with EQ, boost with caution — boosts amplify noise too. Prefer narrow boosts under 3dB
Side-chain EQ: click the small headphone icon to solo the EQ band and hear only that frequency range while sweeping
On your kick: high-pass around 40Hz, add a slight bell boost at 60–80Hz for punch, notch at 200–300Hz if it sounds boxy

images.caption: Click a numbered circle (1–8) at the top to activate a band. Set the filter shape using the icons along the bottom — the leftward-rising curve is high-pass. Double-click the Freq knob and type a number (e.g. 40) to set the cutoff precisely. Drag the dot on the frequency graph up/down to boost or cut.

ableton_version_note: Works in all modern Ableton versions. Spectrum display available in Live 9+
